title: Year 4 Maths: Coordinates, Symmetry and Time
lessonOpener: Every lesson begins with (1) a Working Memory starter activity [generated separately by the working-memory-starters skill] and (2) a Rapid Maths fluency activity [generated separately by the rapid-maths skill]. These are not content-specific to the lesson learning intention.

priorLearning.Coordinates: Y3: right angles, parallel and perpendicular lines, triangles and quadrilaterals classified. Y2: positional language and direction (left/right/up/down, clockwise/anticlockwise), polygons named and described by sides/vertices. CLF RTP 4G-1: first quadrant coordinates and translations.
priorLearning.Symmetry: Y3: classifying triangles and quadrilaterals, right angles identified. Y2: lines of symmetry identified in 2D shapes, polygons sorted by properties. Y3 geometry builds the classification knowledge that underpins recognising symmetry in different shape types.
priorLearning.Time: Y3: time to nearest minute, 24-hour clock introduced, Roman numerals, calculate durations using number lines. Y2: time to 5 minutes, quarter past and quarter to, 12-hour clock. Time is consistently identified as an area requiring significant revisiting of prior content before new learning can proceed.

lessons.li: Pupils give directions from one position to another on a grid.
lessons.cycleNote: Completely new context for most learners — expect to spend longer on Cycle 1 before moving on.

cycle1.focus: Horizontal and vertical moves only
ido: Display a simple labelled 5×5 grid. Model giving directions from one square to another using left/right/up/down and a number of steps.
Narrate aloud: 'I move 3 right, then 2 up.' Show the route on the grid.
Key question: 'Does the order of moves matter? What if I go up first?' Explore with a second example.
wedo: Teacher marks two squares; class write directions on mini-whiteboards. Share and compare.
Sentence stem: 'Move ___ right/left and ___ up/down.'

roles: A: marks start and end
B: writes directions
C: checks by tracing the route
trios.task: Swap roles each round — aim for 3 complete journeys each.
trios.challenge: Can you find two different direction sequences that produce the same journey?

independent.standard: Printed grid with 4 start/end pairs. Write directions for each. Mark the route taken.
independent.supported: Grids with only horizontal or vertical moves — not both combined. Use arrow cards instead of written directions initially.

ido: Giving directions on a grid
Does the order of moves matter?
wedo: Your turn — write the directions

cycle2.focus: Multi-step journeys through three positions
ido: Model a journey that visits three positions in order. Show how to split it into two sets of directions.
Deliberate error: give a direction that lands off the grid. Class identifies what went wrong.

roles: Shared roles from Cycle 1
trios.task: Each trio creates a three-stop journey on a grid, writes the directions for each leg, and swaps with another trio to check.
trios.challenge: Can you find two different sets of directions for the same journey?

independent.standard: Write directions for 3 three-stop journeys on a printed grid. Extension: describe the return journey for one of them.
independent.supported: Two three-stop journeys with one leg already written.

ido: Multi-step journeys — three positions
Spot the mistake

support: Grids with only horizontal or vertical moves. Arrow cards instead of written directions. Sentence frames provided.
stretch: Write directions using positive/negative notation (+3, −2). What does a negative move mean?
Can you describe a route that returns to the starting point? What must be true about the directions?

lp1.task: 4 start/end pairs on a grid. Write directions for each and mark the route.

lp2.task: 3 three-stop journeys. Write directions for each leg. Extension: describe the return journey for one.

learningReviewStems: To give directions on a grid, I...
I found it tricky when...
I could check my directions were right by...

loText.walt: …give directions from one place to another on a grid.
loText.tiob: …being able to describe exactly where something is and how to get there is a skill we use in maps, plans and many other areas of maths.
loText.iwstb: …writing clear directions using left, right, up and down with a number of steps, and checking them by tracing the route.

spotTheMistake.concept: Move lands off the grid — direction count not checked against grid boundary
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: From A at (3,3): move 4 squares right
spotTheMistake.errorNote: 4 right from column 3 lands on column 7 — but the grid only goes to column 6. Always check the grid boundary before writing your directions.

lessons.li: Pupils move objects including polygons on a grid according to directions, and mark the new position.
lessons.cycleNote: Builds directly on lesson 1 — most learners should move through Cycle 1 fairly quickly.

cycle1.focus: Moving a single point, then a simple shape
ido: Place a single dot on a grid. Model moving it: 'Move 4 right, 2 up.' Mark the new position.
Extend to a square: track each corner using the same direction. Draw the square in its new position.
Key question: 'Has the shape changed size or orientation? How do we know?'
wedo: Project a triangle on a grid. Teacher gives a direction; class mark each vertex on mini-whiteboards. Reveal and check.
Common error to expose: moving only one vertex. Show the misshapen result and correct it together.

roles: A: reads direction
B and C: independently move each vertex
trios.task: Printed grids with a rectangle drawn. Direction card given. Compare and resolve any differences.
trios.challenge: Show two different moves that produce the same final position for one vertex but different positions for others. Why does this happen?

independent.standard: Three shapes on a grid. Move each according to the direction given. Draw the new shape in each case.
independent.supported: Shapes with only horizontal or vertical moves. Vertex tracking table: 'Vertex A was here → now here.'

ido: Moving a point, then a shape
What stays the same?
wedo: Your turn — track the vertices

cycle2.focus: Moving polygons with more vertices; reasoning about what stays the same
ido: Show a pentagon. Move it using a two-step direction. Class track all vertices together.
Key question: 'What stays the same about the shape after any move? What would have to happen for the shape to change?'
wedo: Show a pentagon. Move it using a two-step direction. Class track all vertices together on whiteboards.
Key question: 'What stays the same about the shape after any move? What would have to happen for the shape to change?'

roles: Each member tracks different vertices of a polygon
trios.task: Each trio receives a different polygon (4–6 sides). Apply a direction and draw the new position. Write one sentence explaining what stayed the same and why.
trios.challenge: Design a direction that returns the shape exactly to its starting position.

independent.standard: Two more complex polygons to move. Extension: write a direction that would move the shape so one specific vertex lands on a given target square.
independent.supported: One polygon with 4 vertices. Two moves given — complete the first, then the second.

ido: Moving polygons with more vertices
wedo: What stays the same after any move?

support: Shapes with only horizontal or vertical moves. Vertex tracking table provided.
Show two different moves that produce the same final position for one vertex but different for others — why?
stretch: Move a shape so it overlaps its original position. Which squares are shared? Describe the overlap.
Design a direction that returns the shape exactly to its starting position.

lp1.task: Three shapes on a grid, each with a direction instruction. Draw the new position of each shape.

lp2.task: Two more complex polygons to move using two-step directions. Extension: write a direction that lands a specific vertex on a target square.

learningReviewStems: To move a polygon on a grid, I...
I know the shape hasn't changed because...
Something I noticed about moving shapes was...

loText.walt: …move shapes on a grid by following direction instructions.
loText.tiob: …understanding how shapes can be moved without changing what they look like helps us describe and compare shapes in geometry.
loText.iwstb: …drawing a shape in its new position after following a direction, and explaining what stayed the same about the shape.

spotTheMistake.concept: Only one vertex moved when translating a shape — others left in place
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Translate triangle ABC 3 right and 2 up
spotTheMistake.errorNote: Only vertex A has moved. All vertices must move the same distance in the same direction — the whole shape translates, not just one point.

lessons.li: Pupils describe translations of polygons drawn on a square grid.
lessons.cycleNote: Introduces the word 'translation' — spend time on Cycle 1 to establish the vocabulary and counting method.

cycle1.focus: Describing translations involving one direction only
ido: Show a shape in two positions (side by side on the grid). Model how to describe the move: count squares right/left and up/down.
Introduce 'translation': the shape slides without rotating or flipping.
Deliberate error: describe the move as a reflection. Class corrects and explains the difference.
wedo: Show before and after positions involving only horizontal or only vertical movement. Class write descriptions on whiteboards. Share.
Sentence stem: 'The shape has been translated ___ right/left and ___ up/down.'

roles: A: describes the translation
B and C: check by tracking a different vertex each
trios.task: Cards with before/after grids (single-direction moves). Do all three agree?
trios.challenge: Write the inverse of a translation — where would it move the shape back to?

independent.standard: 4 before/after grids (single-direction moves). Write a translation description for each.
independent.supported: Grids where only one direction of movement occurs. Sentence frames provided. Pupils trace the movement with a finger before writing.

ido: What is a translation?
Describing single-direction translations
wedo: Your turn — describe the move

cycle2.focus: Describing translations involving both directions; reasoning about tracking vertices
ido: Model describing a translation with both a horizontal and vertical component. Track one vertex from start to end.
Key question: 'Does it matter which vertex you track? Try tracking a different one.' Confirm the answer is the same.

roles: Each member tracks a different vertex
trios.task: More complex before/after grids. All three should arrive at the same description — discuss any discrepancies.
trios.challenge: Find two translations that together produce the same result as a third. Link this to addition.

independent.standard: 4 more complex grids. Write translation descriptions. Extension: which translations in the set are equivalent? (e.g. 2 right then 1 right = 3 right)
independent.supported: 2 grids with both directions. One vertex already tracked as a starting point.

ido: Translations in two directions

support: Grids where only one direction of movement occurs. Sentence frames. Pupils trace with a finger before writing.
Describe translations using directed number language: right = positive, left = negative.
stretch: Write the inverse of each translation and explain what it means geometrically.
Find two translations that together produce the same result as a third. Link to addition.

lp1.task: 4 before/after grids (single-direction translations). Write a precise description for each.

lp2.task: 4 more complex grids with two-directional translations. Write descriptions. Extension: find equivalent translation pairs.

learningReviewStems: A translation means...
To describe a translation I need to say...
I can check a translation description by...

loText.walt: …describe the movement of shapes on a grid using the word translation.
loText.tiob: …translation is the mathematical word for a sliding movement, and being able to describe it accurately prepares us for working with coordinates.
loText.iwstb: …writing a full description of a translation, saying how far the shape moved right or left and up or down.

spotTheMistake.concept: Translation described in wrong order — vertical before horizontal
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: The shape moved 3 up then 4 right
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The order of moves doesn't change where the shape ends up, but convention is horizontal first then vertical: '4 right and 3 up'. Consistent ordering avoids confusion.

lessons.li: Pupils draw polygons specified by translations.
lessons.cycleNote: Reverses the task from lesson 3 — learners now produce the translated shape rather than describe it.

cycle1.focus: Drawing translations from a simple instruction; one or two vertices
ido: Given a shape and a translation instruction, model drawing the new shape vertex by vertex.
Error to expose: applying the translation to only some vertices. Show the misshapen result.
Key question: 'How do we check the new shape is congruent to the original?'
wedo: Class draws a translated shape together on a shared grid, labelling original vertices A, B, C and new vertices A', B', C'.
Peer-check: measure that each vertex has moved the same distance.

roles: A: reads the instruction
B: moves each vertex
C: checks congruence by comparing side lengths
trios.task: Three shapes with single-direction translation instructions. Rotate roles each shape.
trios.challenge: Predict the new position before drawing, then check.

independent.standard: 3 shapes with single-direction instructions. Draw the translated image and label vertices A/A', B/B', C/C'.
independent.supported: Partially completed diagrams with one vertex of the translated shape already marked. Pupils complete the remaining vertices.

ido: Drawing a translation — vertex by vertex
Spot the error
wedo: Label original and image vertices

cycle2.focus: Drawing translations with two-directional instructions; working backwards
ido: Model a two-directional translation. Stress moving each vertex independently — never move the whole shape by eye.
Reverse task: given the translated image and the instruction, find the original. Model working backwards: 'If it moved 3 right, I reverse by moving 3 left.'
wedo: Class given translated image and instruction. Work backwards together to find and draw the original shape on whiteboards.
Discuss: what operation is the reverse of a translation?

roles: A: draws the translated shape
B: works backwards to find the original
C: verifies both are correct
trios.task: Two-directional instructions. Two rounds — rotate roles.
trios.challenge: Given two shapes on a grid, determine whether one is a translation of the other. Justify.

independent.standard: 3 two-directional translation tasks. For one: draw the translation. For one: work backwards to find the original. For one: determine whether the pair is a valid translation and explain.
independent.supported: Two tasks only. Translation arrows pre-drawn for the first task.

ido: Two-directional translations
Working backwards
wedo: Find the original shape

support: Partially completed diagrams with one vertex already marked.
Translation arrows pre-drawn for guided tasks.
stretch: Given two shapes on a grid, prove algebraically (using coordinates) whether one is a translation of the other.
Combine two translations and find the single equivalent instruction.

lp1.task: 3 shapes with single-direction translation instructions. Draw the translated image and label vertices.

lp2.task: Mixed: draw a translation, work backwards to find an original, identify whether a pair is a valid translation.

learningReviewStems: To draw a translated shape I...
I know my translated shape is correct because...
Working backwards from a translation means...

loText.walt: …draw shapes in their new position after a translation.
loText.tiob: …drawing translations carefully — one corner at a time — builds the accuracy we need when we start working with coordinates.
loText.iwstb: …plotting each new corner correctly, joining them up, and labelling the original shape and the new shape.

spotTheMistake.concept: Translating in the wrong direction — right/left or up/down confused
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Move the shape 3 right and 2 up
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The shape has moved 3 left and 2 up — the horizontal direction is wrong. Right means towards higher column numbers; left means towards lower ones.

lessons.li: Pupils mark points specified as a translation from the origin.
lessons.cycleNote: First encounter with the origin — establish this carefully before introducing full coordinate notation in lesson 6.

cycle1.focus: Locating the origin; moving from it in one direction
ido: Introduce the origin: the point where x-axis and y-axis meet, labelled (0,0). Draw labelled axes on the board.
Model a translation from the origin: 'Start at (0,0). Move 4 right. Mark the point.' Repeat for a point directly up.
Key question: 'What does it mean for a point to be ON the x-axis? ON the y-axis?'
wedo: Teacher calls out translations from the origin (one direction only). Pupils mark points on individual mini grids and label with a letter.
Reverse task: teacher marks a point on the axis; pupils describe the translation from (0,0) to reach it.

roles: A: calls a one-directional translation
B: marks and labels the point
C: describes the translation back from (0,0) to verify
trios.task: 6 one-directional translations. Rotate after each pair.
trios.challenge: Mark four points, all on the axes, that form the corners of a rectangle. Is this possible? Explain.

independent.standard: 6 one-directional translations from the origin. Mark and label each point on a coordinate grid.
independent.supported: Grid with origin clearly labelled and arrows showing right/up. 4 translations, all single direction.

ido: The origin — where axes meet
Moving from the origin
wedo: Mark points on the axes

cycle2.focus: Two-directional translations from the origin
ido: Model a two-directional translation from the origin: 'Move 3 right, 4 up. Mark the point.'
Introduce a structured method: move along the x-axis first, then up. Mark with a cross.
Address misconception: a point can only be on one axis at a time unless it is the origin.
wedo: Teacher calls out 6 two-directional translations. Pupils mark on mini grids and label.
Reverse: teacher marks 4 points; pupils write the translation from the origin for each.

roles: A: calls a two-directional translation
B: plots the point
C: verifies by describing the translation back
trios.task: 8 two-directional translations. Rotate after each.
trios.challenge: Mark 4 points that form a square (not axis-aligned). Write the translation for each vertex from the origin.

independent.standard: 8 two-directional translations from the origin. Mark, label and connect in order — what shape do the points make? Calculate its perimeter.
independent.supported: 5 two-directional translations. Grid with origin labelled. Step-by-step: 'Move ___ right along the bottom, then ___ up.'

ido: Two-directional translations from the origin
wedo: Plot and describe — your turn
independent: Independent — plot, connect, identify the shape

support: Grid with origin clearly labelled and directional arrows. Step-by-step method card.
Start with one-directional translations only before progressing to two-directional.
stretch: Mark 4 points that form a square not aligned to the axes. Write each translation from the origin.
If you scale every translation by 2 (double the steps), what happens to the shape formed?

lp1.task: 6 one-directional translations from the origin. Mark and label each point.

lp2.task: 8 two-directional translations. Mark, label and connect — identify the shape and calculate its perimeter.

learningReviewStems: The origin is the point where...
To plot a point from the origin I...
Today I connected translations and coordinates by...

loText.walt: …mark points on a coordinate grid by moving from the origin.
loText.tiob: …the origin is the starting point for the whole coordinate system, and understanding how to move from it helps us make sense of coordinates.
loText.iwstb: …plotting points accurately from the origin using movements in one or two directions, and labelling each point.

spotTheMistake.concept: Counting steps from the wrong starting point — not from the origin
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Plot the point 3 right and 4 up from the origin
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The point is plotted at (4,5) — the count started from (1,1) instead of the origin (0,0). Always start counting from where the axes cross.

lessons.li: Pupils mark the position of points specified by coordinates in the first quadrant, and write coordinates for already-marked points.
lessons.cycleNote: The key conceptual shift from 'translation from origin' to formal coordinate notation. Take time with the (x,y) convention.

cycle1.focus: Plotting coordinates and reading them from marked points
ido: Introduce coordinate notation: (x, y). Memory aid: 'Along the corridor, then up the stairs.' Draw a first-quadrant grid (0–8 on each axis) with labelled axes.
Model plotting (3,5): count 3 along x-axis, then 5 up. Mark with a cross and label.
Common error: plotting (5,3) when given (3,5). Demonstrate both; pupils identify which is correct and explain why.
wedo: Read out 6 coordinates. Pupils plot on mini-whiteboards and label with a letter. Share — resolve any disagreements by counting together.
Show a grid with 5 points already marked. Pupils write the coordinates. Peer-check.

roles: A: reads a coordinate
B: plots and labels it
C: reads the coordinate back from the marked point to verify
trios.task: 12 coordinates — 6 to plot, 6 to read. Rotate after each set of 3.
trios.challenge: Mark the vertices of a square. Give its coordinates. Verify it is a square by checking side lengths.

independent.standard: Plot 8 given coordinates and label each. Then read 6 marked points and write their coordinates.
independent.supported: Grid with every grid line numbered and a worked example. 5 to plot, 3 to read.

ido: Coordinate notation — (x, y)
Common error: wrong order
wedo: Plot and read — your turn

cycle2.focus: Spotting patterns in coordinates; coordinates on the axes
ido: Mark several points that share an x-coordinate (e.g. (3,1), (3,4), (3,7)). Key question: 'What do you notice? What would a point at (3,0) look like?'
Repeat for shared y-coordinate. Link to the axes: points with y=0 all lie on the x-axis.
wedo: Give a set of coordinates. Pupils sort them into groups sharing an x-coordinate, sharing a y-coordinate, or neither.
Pupils predict: 'If I plot all points where x=5, what will I see?'

roles: A: names a rule (e.g. x=4)
B: generates 4 coordinates that follow the rule
C: plots them and confirms they form a vertical/horizontal line
trios.task: Three rounds with different rules. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Find all coordinates where x + y = 8. What pattern do they make? How many are in the first quadrant?

independent.standard: 8 coordinates to plot; identify which ones share an x or y value and describe the pattern. Extension: find all first-quadrant coordinates where x + y = 6.
independent.supported: 6 coordinates. Pre-sorted into two groups — pupils plot each group and describe what they see.

ido: Patterns in coordinates
wedo: Sort by shared x or y value
trios: Trios — name a rule, generate, plot

support: Grid with every grid line numbered. Worked example with 'corridor then stairs' annotated.
Reduce to 8 coordinates total — 5 to plot, 3 to read.
stretch: Find all coordinates where x + y = 7. What pattern do they make?
Investigate: what happens if x = y? Plot several examples and describe.

lp1.task: Plot 8 given coordinates and label. Read 6 marked points and write their coordinates.

lp2.task: Plot 8 coordinates; identify those sharing an x or y value and describe the pattern. Extension: find all first-quadrant coordinates where x + y = 6.

learningReviewStems: The x-coordinate tells me..., the y-coordinate tells me...
I remember the order of coordinates because...
I spotted a pattern today — when lots of points share an x-coordinate...

loText.walt: …mark the position of points using coordinates, and write the coordinates of points that are already marked.
loText.tiob: …coordinates give us a precise way to describe any position on a grid, and we use them across maths and in the real world.
loText.iwstb: …plotting given coordinates accurately and reading coordinates from marked points, always putting the x-coordinate first.

spotTheMistake.concept: x and y coordinates swapped — reading the y-axis value first
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: The point is at coordinate (4, 2)
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The point shown is at column 2, row 4 — so its coordinate is (2,4), not (4,2). Always read the x-axis (across) first, then the y-axis (up).

lessons.li: Pupils draw polygons specified by coordinates in the first quadrant.
lessons.cycleNote: Combines coordinate plotting with shape knowledge — pupils must identify the polygon type from the coordinates.

cycle1.focus: Plotting and joining coordinates to form triangles and quadrilaterals
ido: Model drawing a triangle: vertices at (1,1), (5,1), (3,4). Plot each vertex, label it, join with straight lines using a ruler.
Ask: 'What type of triangle is this? How can the coordinates help us decide?' Count horizontal and vertical distances between vertices.
Common error: joining vertices in the wrong order, producing a self-intersecting shape. Show example and correct.
wedo: Give 4 coordinates for a quadrilateral. Class plots and joins on mini-whiteboards. Identify the shape type and justify.
Give a partial shape (two vertices plotted, two coordinates given). Pupils complete and name the shape.

roles: A: gives a secret shape name
B: chooses coordinates to match it
C: plots and confirms
trios.task: Three rounds with different shape types. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Use coordinates to create a shape with exactly one line of symmetry. Describe how you know.

independent.standard: Three sets of coordinates. Plot, join, name the polygon, and state one geometric property visible from the coordinates.
independent.supported: Two sets (triangle and rectangle). Pre-drawn grid with axes labelled. Hint: 'Plot each point, then join in order.'

ido: Plot and join — drawing polygons
Spot the error — wrong joining order
wedo: Complete and name the polygon

cycle2.focus: Using coordinates to reason about shape properties
ido: Show a quadrilateral at (1,1), (5,1), (5,4), (1,4). Ask: 'How do I know this is a rectangle without measuring?' (Equal widths and heights from the coordinates.)
Show a parallelogram. Ask: 'Is this a rectangle? What would need to change about the coordinates to make it one?'
wedo: Give pupils three sets of coordinates and ask: 'Which of these, if any, forms a square?' Pupils must justify using coordinates, not just looking at the shape.
Can you find a fourth coordinate that could be added to a triangle to make a parallelogram? Is there more than one answer?

roles: A: proposes coordinates for a shape
B: plots and names it
C: uses coordinates to verify one stated property
trios.task: Three rounds. Final round: trio must create coordinates for a specific shape type given to them on a card.
trios.challenge: Find three different sets of coordinates that all produce the same type of quadrilateral.

independent.standard: Three tasks: identify a shape from coordinates and justify; find a missing fourth vertex to complete a named shape; create coordinates for a specific shape and prove the property using the coordinates.
independent.supported: Two tasks: identify a shape from its coordinates; find one missing vertex of a rectangle given three.

ido: Using coordinates to reason about shape properties
wedo: Which of these forms a square?
independent: Independent — identify, find missing vertex, create

support: Pre-drawn grid with axes labelled. Hint card: 'Plot each point first, then join in order.'
Two sets of coordinates (triangle and rectangle) only.
stretch: Find three different sets of coordinates that all produce the same type of quadrilateral.
Calculate the area of each polygon by counting squares and part-squares.

lp1.task: Three sets of coordinates. Plot, join, name the polygon, state one geometric property.

lp2.task: Identify shapes from coordinates; find missing vertices; create coordinates for a specific shape and prove a property.

learningReviewStems: To draw a polygon from coordinates I...
I identified the shape type by...
I proved a shape property using coordinates by...

loText.walt: …draw polygons on a coordinate grid using a list of vertices.
loText.tiob: …combining coordinate plotting with our knowledge of shapes lets us describe and create them with precision.
loText.iwstb: …plotting the vertices, joining them in order, naming the shape, and identifying one of its properties using the coordinates.

spotTheMistake.concept: Vertices joined in wrong order — shape crosses itself
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Join (1,1), (4,1), (1,4), (4,4) in order to make a square
spotTheMistake.errorNote: Joining these points in that order creates a crossed shape, not a square. To make a square, join adjacent corners: (1,1)→(4,1)→(4,4)→(1,4)→(1,1).

lessons.li: Pupils translate polygons in the first quadrant.
lessons.cycleNote: Brings together coordinate plotting and translation — the key rule is: right increases x, up increases y, left decreases x, down decreases y.

cycle1.focus: Translating a shape by adding to or subtracting from coordinates
ido: Show a triangle at (1,2), (3,2), (2,4). Translation: 3 right, 1 up. Model: add 3 to each x-coordinate, add 1 to each y-coordinate. New vertices: (4,3), (6,3), (5,5).
Key insight: right increases x; up increases y; left decreases x; down decreases y.
Error: a pupil adds the translation to only the x-coordinates. Show the distorted result. Correct together.
wedo: Give a quadrilateral with 4 coordinates and a translation rule. Pupils calculate new coordinates on whiteboards, then plot both shapes. Label originals and images.
Reverse: given the original and translated shape, find the translation rule.

roles: A: calculates new coordinates
B: plots both shapes
C: verifies by measuring that each vertex moved the same distance
trios.task: Two polygons with translation rules. Rotate roles.
trios.challenge: Apply two translations in sequence. Find the single equivalent translation.

independent.standard: Two polygons, each with a translation rule. Calculate new coordinates, draw original and translated shape, label A/A', B/B' etc.
independent.supported: One triangle. Table provided: 'Original vertex | Add to x | Add to y | New vertex.' One row completed as example.

ido: Translating coordinates — the rule
Spot the error
wedo: Calculate, plot and label
independent: Independent — two polygons, label original and image

cycle2.focus: Working backwards from the image; finding translation rules
ido: Show a shape and its translated image. Ask: 'What translation rule maps the original onto the image?' Model working out by comparing corresponding vertices.
Key question: 'Do you need to check all vertices or just one? Why?'
wedo: Show three shape pairs. Pupils find the translation rule for each on whiteboards. One is a deliberate non-translation (the shape has been rotated) — pupils must identify which and explain.
Class discussion: how can you prove two shapes are translations of each other?

roles: A: sets up an original shape and applies a secret translation rule
B: tries to find the rule from the two shapes
C: verifies
trios.task: Two rounds. Rotate roles.
trios.challenge: Shape A is at (1,2), (3,2), (2,4). Shape B is at (4,5), (6,5), (5,7). Are these a translation? If so, what is the rule?

independent.standard: Three pairs of shapes. For each: find the translation rule, calculate a missing vertex in the image, draw the full translated shape.
independent.supported: Two pairs. Translation rule given for the first — pupils verify. Pupils find the rule for the second.

ido: Working backwards — finding the rule
wedo: Find the translation rule
independent: Independent — find rule, missing vertex, draw

support: Table format: 'Original vertex | Add to x | Add to y | New vertex.' One row completed as example.
One shape only; single-direction translation.
stretch: Investigate: does the order of two translations matter? Apply AB then BA to the same shape.
Find a translation that maps polygon A exactly onto polygon B when both are on the same grid.

lp1.task: Two polygons with translation rules. Calculate new coordinates, draw and label original and image.

lp2.task: Three pairs of shapes. Find the translation rule; calculate a missing vertex; draw the full translated shape.

learningReviewStems: To translate a polygon using coordinates I...
Right increases x because...
I can find a translation rule by...

loText.walt: …translate shapes in the first quadrant using coordinates.
loText.tiob: …understanding how translations change coordinates is an important link between geometry and number.
loText.iwstb: …calculating the new coordinates after a translation, drawing both the original and the new shape, and labelling each vertex.

spotTheMistake.concept: Only x-coordinate updated when translating — y-coordinate unchanged
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Translate point (2,3) by 3 right and 2 up. New position: (5,3)
spotTheMistake.errorNote: (5,3) is wrong — only the x-coordinate was updated. Both coordinates change: x increases by 3 and y increases by 2, giving (5,5).

lessons.li: Pupils mark points specified as a translation from the origin AND draw polygons from coordinates — consolidating and connecting lessons 5–8.
lessons.cycleNote: Consolidation and connection lesson — bringing together the skills of the previous four lessons before moving to symmetry.

cycle1.focus: Fluent plotting and reading; identifying translations between shape pairs
ido: Quick recap — teacher plots three points and reads three back, narrating the process aloud. Class confirms each. Stress accuracy with the (x,y) convention.
Show two shapes on the same grid. Ask: 'Is shape B a translation of shape A? How can we prove it?' Model checking corresponding vertices.
wedo: Three tasks on whiteboards: plot a set of coordinates and name the polygon; find a missing vertex; identify the translation rule between two given shapes.
Pupils compare approaches — did everyone use the same method? Was one more efficient?

roles: A: plots a shape from a given set of coordinates
B: applies a translation rule and draws the image
C: verifies and writes the translation description
trios.task: Two complete cycles. Rotate roles.
trios.challenge: Create a shape and translation such that the original and image together form a new, larger recognisable shape.

independent.standard: Plot a polygon from coordinates, apply a given translation rule, label original and image, state the translation description.
independent.supported: Plot a triangle from coordinates. One vertex of the translated image already marked — complete the rest.

ido: Connecting plotting and translations
wedo: Three tasks — plot, find a vertex, identify the rule
independent: Independent — full coordinate and translation task

cycle2.focus: Problem-solving with coordinates and translations
ido: Present a multi-step problem: 'Shape A has vertices at (2,1), (4,1), (3,3). It is translated so that vertex (2,1) moves to (5,4). What are the new coordinates of the other vertices?'
Model the process: find the translation rule first, then apply to all vertices.
wedo: Three similar multi-step problems on whiteboards. Class works in pairs first, then shares.
Key question: 'What is the minimum information you need to fully describe a translation?'

roles: Problem setter (creates a coordinate and translation challenge)
Solver (works through it methodically)
Checker (verifies using the inverse)
trios.task: Two rounds. Rotate roles.
trios.challenge: Design a coordinate challenge for another group that requires them to use both plotting and translation skills.

independent.standard: Three multi-step problems: find a translation rule from partial information, apply it, and verify.
independent.supported: Two problems with structured working frames. Translation rule given for one.

ido: Multi-step coordinate problems
wedo: Work through it together

support: All plotting tasks on grids with numbered grid lines. One vertex pre-plotted for translation tasks.
Structured working frames for multi-step problems.
stretch: Design a coordinate challenge for another group requiring both plotting and translation.
Investigate: can a shape ever be its own translation (i.e. the original and image coincide)?

lp1.task: Plot a polygon from coordinates, apply a translation, label original and image, state the description.

lp2.task: Three multi-step problems: find a translation rule from partial information, apply it, verify.

learningReviewStems: I can connect plotting coordinates and translations because...
The most efficient method I used today was...
I checked my translation was correct by...

loText.walt: …use coordinate plotting and translation together to solve problems.
loText.tiob: …being able to move fluently between plotting, reading and translating prepares us for more challenging geometry problems.
loText.iwstb: …completing a multi-step problem that uses both coordinate plotting and translation, showing clear working.

spotTheMistake.concept: Translation rule stated in reverse — subtracting instead of adding
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Shape moved from A(1,2) to B(4,5). Translation rule: subtract 3 from x and 3 from y
spotTheMistake.errorNote: x went from 1 to 4 — that's adding 3, not subtracting. y went from 2 to 5 — also adding 3. The rule is: add 3 to x and add 3 to y.

lessons.li: Pupils translate polygons in the first quadrant — problem-solving and reasoning with coordinates and translations.
lessons.cycleNote: Final coordinates lesson — focus on reasoning and justification rather than procedure. Prepares pupils for the shift to symmetry.

cycle1.focus: Translating with negative components (moving left or down); reasoning about constraints
ido: Recap: a translation moving left or down decreases coordinates. Model: 'Translate 2 left, 3 down — subtract 2 from x, subtract 3 from y.'
Key constraint: a shape in the first quadrant cannot be translated so far left or down that any vertex leaves the quadrant (coordinates become negative). Show an example that goes out of bounds.
Key question: 'Given a shape at (4,3), (6,3), (5,5), what is the maximum translation left before a vertex leaves the first quadrant?'
wedo: Three translation tasks involving moving left or down. Pupils calculate new coordinates and check all remain non-negative.
Pupils find the 'boundary translation' — the furthest left/down they can go while keeping all vertices in the first quadrant.

roles: A: proposes a translation (any direction)
B: applies it and checks it stays in the first quadrant
C: if it goes out of bounds, finds the maximum valid translation in that direction
trios.task: Three rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Find the translation that moves a shape as close to the origin as possible without any vertex leaving the first quadrant.

independent.standard: Three shapes with translations (some involving left/down). Calculate new coordinates, draw the result, flag any that would leave the first quadrant and adjust.
independent.supported: Two shapes, translations right and up only. Sentence frame: 'This translation is valid because all coordinates are ___.''

ido: Translating left and down — constraints
Maximum valid translation
wedo: Calculate and check — stay in the quadrant

cycle2.focus: Reasoning: composite translations, inverse translations, equivalence
ido: Model two successive translations on the same shape. Ask: 'What single translation is equivalent to these two combined?'
Show that translation A then translation B gives the same result as translation B then translation A. Ask: 'Does order matter for translations? Will this always be true?'
wedo: Pairs work out the combined effect of given translation pairs. Share results — do all pairs agree?
Key question: 'What is the inverse of a translation? How do you find it?'

roles: A: applies two successive translations to a shape
B: finds the single equivalent translation
C: applies the equivalent translation to the original and confirms the shapes match
trios.task: Two rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Apply three successive translations. Find the single equivalent. What would happen with four, five, n translations?

independent.standard: Three tasks: find the combined effect of two translations; find the inverse of a given translation; determine whether two composite translations are equivalent.
independent.supported: Two tasks. Combined translation: given — pupils verify by applying both steps separately.

ido: Composite and inverse translations
wedo: Find the combined effect
trios: Trios — apply two, find the equivalent

support: Translations right and up only for Cycle 1. Sentence frames for justification.
Structured working frames for composite translations in Cycle 2.
stretch: Apply three successive translations and find the single equivalent. Generalise for n translations.
Investigate: is it possible to return a shape to its original position using only two translations? What must be true about them?

lp1.task: Three shapes with translations involving left/down. Calculate new coordinates; flag any going out of bounds.

lp2.task: Composite and inverse translations. Find combined effects; find inverses; determine equivalence.

learningReviewStems: A translation moving left or down...
I checked a shape stayed in the first quadrant by...
Two translations combined are equivalent to...

loText.walt: …reason about translations, including movements left and down, and find combined and reverse translations.
loText.tiob: …thinking carefully about what translations do to coordinates, and how they combine, develops our mathematical reasoning.
loText.iwstb: …finding the combined effect of two translations, finding a reverse translation, and explaining why a shape can or cannot reach a given position.

spotTheMistake.concept: Composite translation calculated additively but applied as separate moves
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Move 3 right then 2 left. Combined move: 5 right
spotTheMistake.errorNote: Moving 3 right then 2 left cancels some movement. The net effect is 3 − 2 = 1 right, not 5 right. Combine translations by adding signed values: +3 and −2 gives +1.

lessons.li: Pupils complete a symmetrical pattern and compose symmetrical shapes from two congruent shapes.
lessons.cycleNote: Opening symmetry lesson — Cycle 1 establishes the core concept of a line of symmetry through pattern completion. Cycle 2 extends this to composing shapes.

cycle1.focus: Completing symmetrical patterns on a grid
ido: Draw a vertical line of symmetry on a grid. Colour a pattern of 5 squares on the left side. Model completing the right side: count the distance from each coloured square to the mirror line, mark the same distance on the other side.
Key language: line of symmetry, mirror line, reflect, corresponding, equidistant.
Error example: a 'completed' pattern where one square is one step too far from the line. Pupils spot and correct it.
wedo: Display a half-pattern with a vertical line of symmetry. Pupils complete the other half on mini-whiteboards. Place a mirror along the line to check.
Repeat with a horizontal line of symmetry — same process, different orientation.

roles: A: creates a half-pattern (up to 8 squares)
B: completes the symmetrical half
C: checks with a mirror
trios.task: Three rounds — mix of vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Create a half-pattern that produces a recognisable letter when reflected.

independent.standard: Four grids, each showing a half-pattern and a line of symmetry (mix of vertical and horizontal). Complete each pattern.
independent.supported: Three grids. Line of symmetry drawn in red. Patterns of no more than 4 squares on one side. Mirror provided.

ido: Completing symmetrical patterns
Spot the error — wrong distance
wedo: Complete the half-pattern
trios: Trios — create, complete, check with mirror

cycle2.focus: Composing symmetrical shapes from two congruent shapes
ido: Show two congruent right-angled triangles (card cut-outs). Place them together along the hypotenuse — make a rectangle. Draw it and mark the line of symmetry.
Now join along a shorter edge. What shape? Is it symmetrical? Model checking by folding.
Vocabulary: congruent (same shape and size). Ask: 'Is the join line always a line of symmetry?'
wedo: Pairs receive two congruent triangles. In how many ways can you join them? Draw each result and mark lines of symmetry (if any). Share findings.
Repeat with two congruent rectangles. How many distinct symmetrical compositions are possible?

roles: A: arranges the two shapes
B: draws the composite shape
C: identifies and marks lines of symmetry
trios.task: Three different congruent shape pairs. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Using two congruent L-shaped hexominoes, find a symmetrical composition.

independent.standard: Four pairs of congruent shapes. Draw one symmetrical composition for each and mark the line(s) of symmetry. Explain in one sentence why it is symmetrical.
independent.supported: Two pairs. Physical cut-outs available. Sentence starter: 'The shape is symmetrical because...'

ido: Composing symmetrical shapes from congruent pieces
wedo: How many ways can you join them?
trios: Trios — arrange, draw, identify lines of symmetry

support: Mirror and red line of symmetry for pattern completion. Cut-outs for composing task.
Reduce to patterns of 4 squares; two pairs of shapes only.
stretch: Create a pattern with both vertical AND horizontal lines of symmetry.
Can two congruent shapes always be joined to make a symmetrical shape? Investigate and explain.

lp1.task: Four half-patterns to complete (mix of vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry).

lp2.task: Four pairs of congruent shapes. Draw one symmetrical composition for each and explain why it is symmetrical.

learningReviewStems: A line of symmetry is...
To complete a symmetrical pattern I...
When I joined two congruent shapes I found...

loText.walt: …complete symmetrical patterns and make symmetrical shapes by joining two matching pieces.
loText.tiob: …understanding symmetry helps us describe and create shapes precisely, and we can see it all around us in the real world.
loText.iwstb: …completing a half-pattern accurately across a mirror line, and drawing a symmetrical shape from two identical pieces.

spotTheMistake.concept: Reflected point placed at wrong distance from mirror line
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Reflect point A across the vertical mirror line
spotTheMistake.errorNote: A is 2 squares from the mirror line, so its reflection must also be 2 squares on the other side — not 3. Count carefully from the line, not from the edge.

lessons.li: Pupils investigate lines of symmetry in 2D shapes by folding paper shape cut-outs, and find lines of symmetry using a mirror.
lessons.cycleNote: Investigation lesson — pupils generate their own findings systematically. Cycle 2 extends to diagonal lines of symmetry, which pupils often miss.

cycle1.focus: Finding lines of symmetry by folding — systematic investigation
ido: Hold up a square cut-out. Fold it in half four different ways — record which folds produce exact matches. The square has 4 lines of symmetry.
Repeat with an equilateral triangle (3 lines), regular pentagon (5 lines), scalene triangle (0 lines). Build a table: shape → number of lines.
Ask: 'What pattern do you notice about regular polygons?' (Number of lines = number of sides.)
wedo: Pairs receive cut-outs: rectangle, isosceles triangle, regular hexagon, kite, parallelogram. Fold and record on a shared table. Class compiles results.
Key question: 'Why does a rectangle have only 2 lines, not 4? Why does a parallelogram have 0?'

roles: A: folds the cut-out
B: marks the fold line on a drawing
C: records the total and reports to the class
trios.task: Five shapes, one per round. Rotate shapes between groups.
trios.challenge: Find a shape with exactly 1 line of symmetry and explain why it has exactly one.

independent.standard: A table of 8 shapes. Draw all lines of symmetry on each and record the total. Shapes: square, rectangle, equilateral triangle, isosceles triangle, scalene triangle, regular hexagon, kite, parallelogram.
independent.supported: Five shapes. Dotted fold-line guides on half of them. Partially completed table.

ido: Finding lines of symmetry by folding
Regular polygons — the pattern
wedo: Fold, mark, record — five shapes

cycle2.focus: Finding diagonal lines of symmetry using a mirror
ido: Explain that lines of symmetry can be diagonal, not just horizontal or vertical. Draw a square. Use a mirror to demonstrate all four lines — including the two diagonals.
Show a rhombus — it has 2 lines of symmetry, both diagonal. Show a rectangle — it has 2 lines of symmetry, neither diagonal.
Error: a pupil claims a parallelogram has diagonal lines of symmetry. Demonstrate with a mirror that it does not — the halves do not match.
wedo: Give pairs a set of shape cards and mirrors. They test each shape — horizontal, vertical, both diagonals — and record.
Class discussion: which types of shapes tend to have diagonal lines of symmetry? (Squares, rhombuses, regular polygons.)

roles: A: places the mirror on the shape
B: marks the line if confirmed as a line of symmetry
C: verifies by examining the reflected image
trios.task: Six shapes. Rotate after each.
trios.challenge: Design a shape that has exactly one diagonal line of symmetry and no horizontal or vertical ones.

independent.standard: Six shapes. Use a ruler to draw all lines of symmetry on each (including diagonals). Record the total. Include shapes where diagonals are/are not lines of symmetry.
independent.supported: Three shapes. Large printed shapes. Horizontal and vertical already marked — focus on testing diagonals only.

ido: Diagonal lines of symmetry — using a mirror
Common error — the parallelogram
wedo: Test horizontal, vertical and diagonal

support: Cut-outs and mirrors for both cycles. Partially completed tables for Cycle 1.
Large printed shapes with horizontal and vertical pre-marked for Cycle 2.
stretch: Investigate: can a shape have more diagonal lines of symmetry than horizontal/vertical ones?
Is it possible for a shape to have an infinite number of lines of symmetry? What shape?

lp1.task: Table of 8 shapes. Draw all lines of symmetry and record the total for each.

lp2.task: Six shapes. Draw all lines of symmetry including diagonals. Record totals and justify any you are unsure about.

learningReviewStems: A regular polygon with n sides has... lines of symmetry because...
I was surprised that... because I expected...
A diagonal line of symmetry means...

loText.walt: …find all the lines of symmetry in 2D shapes, including diagonal ones.
loText.tiob: …finding lines of symmetry carefully and systematically, including diagonal ones, deepens our understanding of how shapes are made.
loText.iwstb: …drawing all the lines of symmetry on a set of shapes, including any diagonal lines, and recording the correct total for each shape.

spotTheMistake.concept: Parallelogram claimed to have a line of symmetry along its diagonal
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: This parallelogram has a line of symmetry along the diagonal
spotTheMistake.errorNote: A parallelogram has no lines of symmetry. Folding along the diagonal does not produce two matching halves — the angles and side lengths don't align. Only rectangles and rhombuses (special cases) have diagonals as lines of symmetry.

lessons.li: Pupils reflect polygons in a line of symmetry.
lessons.cycleNote: The shift from identifying to constructing reflections. Cycle 1 uses lines along grid lines (vertical and horizontal). Cycle 2 introduces the diagonal.

cycle1.focus: Reflecting polygons in vertical and horizontal mirror lines
ido: Draw a triangle on a grid with a vertical mirror line to its right. Model reflecting each vertex: count the perpendicular distance from each vertex to the mirror line, mark the same distance on the other side. Join the new vertices.
Emphasise: the reflected shape is congruent — same size, same shape, flipped. The distance from each point to the mirror line is preserved.
Error: a pupil flips the shape but also moves it away from the mirror line. Show and correct.
wedo: Display a quadrilateral with a horizontal mirror line. Pupils reflect it on mini-whiteboards. Check: does each vertex sit the same number of squares from the line on both sides?
Second example — vertical mirror line, more complex polygon. Pupils check peers' work.

roles: A: draws the reflected shape
B: measures that each vertex is equidistant from the line
C: labels original vertices A, B, C and reflected vertices A', B', C'
trios.task: Three polygons with different mirror line positions (all vertical or horizontal). Rotate.
trios.challenge: Predict where the reflected shape will be before drawing — check after.

independent.standard: Three polygons, each with a mirror line (two vertical, one horizontal). Reflect accurately and label the reflected vertices.
independent.supported: Two triangles. Mirror line marked in red. Counting frame: 'Vertex A is ___ squares from the line, so A' is ___ squares on the other side.'

ido: Reflecting in vertical and horizontal mirror lines
Spot the error — shape moved away from line
wedo: Reflect the quadrilateral
trios: Trios — three polygons, three mirror lines

cycle2.focus: Reflecting polygons in a diagonal mirror line (45°)
ido: Draw a right-angled triangle with vertices at grid intersections. Mirror line is the 45° diagonal (y = x line). Model reflecting: swap x and y coordinates of each vertex.
Key insight for a 45° line: if vertex is at (a,b), the reflection is at (b,a). Demonstrate with an example.
Compare with horizontal/vertical reflections — the method differs. Ask pupils to articulate why.
wedo: Class reflects two shapes in a 45° mirror line on whiteboards. Swap coordinates to find reflected vertices, then plot.
Key question: 'How is this different from reflecting in a vertical line? What stays the same?'

roles: A: identifies the coordinates of each vertex
B: applies the reflection rule
C: plots the reflected shape and checks
trios.task: Two shapes reflected in a diagonal mirror line. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Reflect a shape in the diagonal and then reflect the image in a vertical line. Describe the combined transformation.

independent.standard: Two shapes reflected in a 45° diagonal mirror line. Calculate reflected coordinates, draw the image, label vertices.
independent.supported: One triangle. Diagonal mirror line pre-drawn. One vertex already reflected as an example.

ido: Reflecting in a diagonal mirror line
The coordinate swap rule
wedo: Swap coordinates — reflect on the diagonal

support: Mirror line marked in red for all tasks. Counting frame for Cycle 1. One vertex pre-reflected for Cycle 2.
Vertical and horizontal mirror lines only in Cycle 1 (omit Cycle 2 if not ready).
stretch: Explore what happens when you reflect a shape in two perpendicular mirror lines.
Reflect a shape in the diagonal then in a vertical — describe the combined effect.

lp1.task: Three polygons reflected in vertical and horizontal mirror lines. Draw and label the reflected vertices.

lp2.task: Two shapes reflected in a 45° diagonal mirror line. Calculate reflected coordinates, draw and label.

learningReviewStems: To reflect a polygon accurately I...
I know a reflected vertex is in the right place because...
Reflecting in a diagonal line is different because...

loText.walt: …reflect shapes across a mirror line.
loText.tiob: …reflection is a precise transformation — every point must end up the same distance from the mirror line on the other side.
loText.iwstb: …drawing the reflected shape accurately, labelling matching vertices, and checking that each point is the same distance from the mirror line on both sides.

spotTheMistake.concept: Reflection moves the shape parallel to the mirror line instead of perpendicular
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Reflect triangle across the vertical mirror line at x=4
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The triangle has been moved sideways (parallel to the mirror line) rather than flipped through it. Reflection is always perpendicular to the mirror line — each point moves straight across, not along.

lessons.li: Pupils reflect polygons that are dissected by a line of symmetry.
lessons.cycleNote: The most conceptually demanding symmetry lesson — the mirror line passes through the shape itself. Take time with the key distinction: vertices on the line stay fixed.

cycle1.focus: Shapes where the mirror line passes through one vertex
ido: Draw a triangle with one vertex on a vertical mirror line and two vertices on the left side. Ask: 'Which vertex stays fixed? Why?'
Model: the vertex on the line stays fixed. Reflect the two off-line vertices. Join to produce the reflected image.
Show the result: the original and image share the on-line vertex. Together they form a larger symmetrical shape.
wedo: Class given two triangles where the mirror line passes through one vertex. Identify the fixed vertex, reflect the others, draw the composite shape on whiteboards.
Key question: 'Is the composite shape always symmetrical? Check with a mirror.'

roles: A: identifies which vertices are on/off the line
B: applies the reflection
C: checks the composite shape has the mirror line as a line of symmetry
trios.task: Three shapes, each with the mirror line passing through one vertex. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Design a straddling shape where the composite result is a regular polygon.

independent.standard: Three triangles with the mirror line through one vertex. Reflect to produce the complete composite shape. Label all vertices.
independent.supported: Two shapes. Mirror line marked in red. Vertex on the line pre-marked with a dot.

ido: Shapes where the mirror line passes through a vertex
wedo: Identify fixed vertex — reflect the others
independent: Independent — three triangles, one vertex on the line

cycle2.focus: Shapes where the mirror line passes through an edge or multiple vertices
ido: Draw a quadrilateral that straddles a vertical mirror line — two vertices on each side, none on the line itself.
Model: reflect all four vertices. The result is an 8-sided composite shape.
Show a case where one entire edge lies on the mirror line. Ask: 'What happens to that edge in the reflection?' (It stays in place — it is shared by both halves.)
wedo: Two shapes straddling a horizontal mirror line — one with no vertices on the line, one with an edge on the line. Pupils draw the full composite on whiteboards.
Discussion: how does the area of the composite relate to the area of the original?

roles: A: identifies vertices on/off the line and any edges on the line
B: applies the reflection
C: verifies the composite is symmetrical
trios.task: Two complex straddling shapes. Rotate.
trios.challenge: If the original shape has area 12 square units and straddles the mirror line, is the composite always 24 square units? Investigate.

independent.standard: Three straddling polygons (mix of vertices on the line, edges on the line, neither). Reflect to produce the complete composite shape. Label all vertices.
independent.supported: Two shapes: one with a vertex on the line, one with no vertices on the line. Worked example at the top of the LP for the second type.

ido: Mirror line through an edge or multiple vertices
wedo: Draw the full composite shape
independent: Independent — three straddling polygons, mixed types

support: Vertical mirror lines only. Mirror line marked in red. Vertex on the line pre-identified with a dot.
Worked example at top of LP for the more complex type.
stretch: Reflect a shape that straddles a diagonal mirror line.
What types of polygon produce a composite shape that is itself a regular polygon?

lp1.task: Three triangles with mirror line through one vertex. Reflect and draw the composite shape.

lp2.task: Three straddling polygons (mixed types). Reflect to produce the composite symmetrical shape.

learningReviewStems: When the mirror line passes through a vertex...
I found the fixed point by...
The composite shape was symmetrical because...

loText.walt: …reflect shapes where the mirror line passes through the shape itself.
loText.tiob: …when the mirror line goes through a shape, we need to think carefully about which points move and which stay in place.
loText.iwstb: …identifying which vertices are on the mirror line and which are not, completing the reflection, and drawing the full symmetrical shape.

spotTheMistake.concept: Vertex on the mirror line incorrectly moved during reflection
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Reflect the triangle — vertex C sits on the mirror line
spotTheMistake.errorNote: Vertex C is on the mirror line, so it stays exactly where it is — it doesn't move. Only points away from the line get reflected. A fixed point is its own reflection.

lessons.li: Pupils find lines of symmetry in 2D shapes and reflect polygons — consolidation and problem-solving.
lessons.cycleNote: Consolidation lesson — mix of identification and construction. Focus on justification and reasoning rather than procedure.

cycle1.focus: Identifying lines of symmetry and justifying
ido: Display five shapes and ask pupils to predict the number of lines of symmetry before checking. Reveal answers — focus on any surprises.
Key question: 'How can we prove a line IS a line of symmetry without folding or using a mirror?'
wedo: Pairs given four shapes. For each: write the number of lines of symmetry, draw them, and write one sentence justifying why each is a genuine line of symmetry.
Share — class compares justifications. Which are precise? Which are vague?

roles: A: draws a shape (regular or irregular)
B: finds and marks all lines of symmetry
C: challenges or confirms each line with a justification
trios.task: Three rounds. A must draw at least one regular and one irregular shape across the rounds.
trios.challenge: Draw a shape with exactly 3 lines of symmetry that is NOT a regular triangle.

independent.standard: Six shapes. Draw all lines of symmetry and write one sentence justifying each. Include at least one shape with no lines of symmetry — explain why.
independent.supported: Four shapes. Lines of symmetry to identify and draw. Sentence starter provided.

ido: Identifying and justifying lines of symmetry
wedo: Draw, count, justify

cycle2.focus: Mixed reflection problems — standard and straddling
ido: Rapid model: reflect a standard polygon (no straddling), then a straddling polygon. Narrate the decision at each step — 'Is this vertex on the line? Off the line?'
Present a reflection with a deliberate error (wrong distance from mirror line). Pupils identify and correct.
wedo: Four mixed reflection tasks on whiteboards (standard and straddling). Pupils complete and label. Peer-check.
Discussion: 'What is your method for checking a reflection is correct? How do you know you haven't made an error?'

roles: A: sets a reflection task (standard or straddling)
B: completes the reflection
C: checks and either approves or corrects with explanation
trios.task: Three rounds. A must include at least one straddling task.
trios.challenge: Complete a reflection where the mirror line is diagonal and the shape straddles it.

independent.standard: Four mixed reflection tasks (two standard, two straddling). For one task: identify and correct a given erroneous reflection.
independent.supported: Three tasks — two standard reflections, one straddling with one vertex on the line. Mirror line marked in red.

ido: Mixed reflections — standard and straddling
Spot and correct the error
wedo: Four mixed reflection tasks
independent: Independent — four mixed tasks, one error to correct

support: Sentence starters for justification. Mirror available. Red mirror lines.
Straddling reflections: vertical lines only, one vertex on the line.
stretch: Draw a shape with exactly 3 lines of symmetry that is not a regular triangle.
Complete a reflection where the mirror line is diagonal and the shape straddles it.

lp1.task: Six shapes. Draw all lines of symmetry and write one justifying sentence for each.

lp2.task: Four mixed reflection tasks (standard and straddling). Identify and correct one erroneous reflection.

learningReviewStems: I proved a line was a line of symmetry by...
The difference between a standard reflection and a straddling reflection is...
My checking method for reflections is...

loText.walt: …identify lines of symmetry and explain how we know, and complete a range of reflections.
loText.tiob: …being able to explain why a line is a line of symmetry, not just spot it, shows that we really understand what symmetry means.
loText.iwstb: …drawing all lines of symmetry with a written explanation for each, and completing both straightforward and more challenging reflections accurately.

spotTheMistake.concept: Claiming a shape has more lines of symmetry than it actually has
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: A rectangle has 4 lines of symmetry — through corners and midpoints
spotTheMistake.errorNote: A rectangle only has 2 lines of symmetry — through the midpoints of opposite sides. The diagonal lines do NOT produce matching halves because the two sides have different lengths.

lessons.li: Pupils apply all symmetry knowledge — reasoning about symmetry, reflections and composition.
lessons.cycleNote: Final symmetry lesson — reasoning and generalisation. Pupils should be explaining their thinking, not just completing tasks.

cycle1.focus: Generalising about lines of symmetry in polygon families
ido: Display a table showing regular polygons from triangle to octagon with their number of lines of symmetry. Ask: 'What is the pattern? Can you predict for a regular 12-sided polygon?'
Ask: 'What about irregular versions of the same polygons — why do most have fewer lines of symmetry?'
wedo: Class generalises: 'A regular n-sided polygon has ___ lines of symmetry.' Test the generalisation for a circle.
Discussion: Is a circle a regular polygon? What does regular mean?

roles: A: proposes a generalisation about symmetry
B: tests it with two examples
C: tries to find a counter-example
trios.task: Three generalisations. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Write a generalisation about irregular quadrilaterals and lines of symmetry. Test it.

independent.standard: Five reasoning questions about lines of symmetry in different shape families. Write a generalisation, test it, identify exceptions.
independent.supported: Three reasoning questions with sentence starters and partially completed examples.

ido: Generalising lines of symmetry in polygon families
wedo: Test the generalisation — does it hold?
trios: Trios — propose, test, find a counter-example

cycle2.focus: Applying reflections to solve problems and create designs
ido: Show a design challenge: 'Using only two reflections of this shape, create a composite design with 4 lines of symmetry.' Model the first reflection, then ask pupils to predict the second.
Key question: 'What must be true about the two mirror lines for the composite to have 4 lines of symmetry?'
wedo: Pairs attempt the design challenge. Share — compare different solutions. Did everyone reach the same design?
Discussion: 'If two mirror lines are perpendicular, how many lines of symmetry does the composite have?'

roles: A: chooses a starting shape and a number of target lines of symmetry
B: applies the reflections to achieve the target
C: verifies the target has been met
trios.task: Two design challenges. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Create a design with exactly 6 lines of symmetry using reflections of a starting shape.

independent.standard: Two design challenges and two proof tasks: given a composite design, prove it has the claimed number of lines of symmetry.
independent.supported: One design challenge with the first reflection already completed. One proof task with sentence starters.

ido: Using reflections to create symmetrical designs
wedo: Design challenge — four lines of symmetry

support: Sentence starters for generalisations and proofs. First reflection pre-completed for design tasks.
Reduce to three reasoning questions with partially completed tables.
stretch: Create a design with exactly 6 lines of symmetry using reflections of a starting shape.
Investigate: what is the minimum number of reflections needed to produce a shape with n lines of symmetry?

lp1.task: Five reasoning questions about lines of symmetry — generalise, test, identify exceptions.

lp2.task: Two design challenges using reflections; two proof tasks about lines of symmetry in composite designs.
