loText.walt: …spot patterns in the lines of symmetry of different shape families, and use reflections to create symmetrical designs.
loText.tiob: …finding patterns across different shapes and testing whether they always work is an important part of mathematical thinking.
loText.iwstb: …writing and testing a pattern about lines of symmetry, and creating a design that has a specific number of lines of symmetry.

spotTheMistake.concept: Reflected shape is the correct shape but placed on the wrong side of the mirror line
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: The shape has been reflected in the mirror line at y=4
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The image has been placed on the same side as the original — not across the mirror line. After reflecting, the image must always appear on the opposite side of the line from the original.

lessons.li: Read, write and convert time between analogue and digital 12-hour and 24-hour clocks.
lessons.cycleNote: Time requires significant revisiting of prior knowledge. Cycle 1 re-establishes 12-hour reading before introducing 24-hour. Do not rush through prior content — this is where errors embed themselves.

cycle1.focus: Reading analogue clocks and converting to 12-hour digital — revisiting prior learning
ido: Draw analogue clocks showing o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to, and to-the-minute times. Read each aloud and model writing as 12-hour digital.
Focus on common errors: confusing the hour and minute hands; forgetting 'quarter to' is 45 minutes past, not 15 minutes to.
Key question: 'What is the difference between 7:45am and 7:45pm? Show both on an analogue clock.'
wedo: Show 8 analogue clock images (mix of am and pm times). Pupils write the 12-hour digital time on whiteboards. Resolve any disagreements by counting minute marks.
Reverse: read 8 digital times aloud — pupils draw the analogue clock on mini clock faces.

roles: A: shows a time card (analogue)
B: writes the 12-hour digital equivalent
C: confirms whether am or pm is possible and why
trios.task: 10 analogue time cards. Rotate after every 4.
trios.challenge: Sort 12 times into chronological order across a 24-hour day — using a mix of analogue images and 12-hour digital times.

independent.standard: 8 analogue clocks — write each as a 12-hour digital time (am or pm given). 4 digital times — draw the analogue clock.
independent.supported: 6 analogue clocks (o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to only). Write as 12-hour digital. Blank clock faces and minute-mark grid provided.

ido: Reading analogue clocks
am and pm — what's the difference?
wedo: Write the 12-hour digital time
trios: Trios — time cards, am or pm?
independent: Independent — analogue to digital and back

cycle2.focus: Converting between 12-hour and 24-hour digital times
ido: Explain 24-hour time. Draw a number line from 00:00 to 23:59 with 12:00 midday marked. Show that am times are the same in 24-hour (e.g. 09:15 = 9:15am). Show that pm times add 12 (e.g. 3:45pm = 15:45).
Special cases: 12:00 midday is 12:00 in 24-hour. 12:00 midnight is 00:00. Model both carefully.
Common error: writing 1:00pm as 13:00am. Stress: 24-hour times above 12:00 are always pm — the term 'am/pm' is not used in 24-hour format at all.
wedo: 12 conversion tasks on whiteboards — a mix of: 12-hour am/pm → 24-hour; 24-hour → 12-hour am/pm. Include the tricky cases around noon and midnight.
Pairs create their own tricky example for another pair. Share two with the class.

roles: A: shows a 24-hour time card
B: writes the 12-hour equivalent
C: confirms — is it am or pm? How do you know?
trios.task: 12 time cards (mix of formats). Rotate after every 4.
trios.challenge: Find all times where the 12-hour and 24-hour times look identical. How many are there and why?

independent.standard: Conversion table — 12 times in one format, convert to both other formats. Include times around noon and midnight.
independent.supported: 8 conversions. Number line 00:00–23:59 with am/pm zones marked. Conversion rule card provided. Excludes midnight and noon edge cases.

ido: Converting to 24-hour time
Special cases — noon and midnight
wedo: 12 conversion tasks on whiteboards

support: Minute-mark grid and blank clock faces for Cycle 1. Number line and conversion rule card for Cycle 2.
Cycle 1: o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to only. Cycle 2: exclude midnight and noon edge cases.
stretch: Find all times where the 12-hour and 24-hour times look identical. Why does this happen?
Write a 24-hour timetable for your ideal school day. Calculate total time in each lesson.

lp1.task: 8 analogue → 12-hour digital conversions; 4 digital → analogue clock drawings.

lp2.task: Conversion table: 12 times in one format, convert to both others. Include noon and midnight edge cases.

learningReviewStems: The difference between 12-hour and 24-hour time is...
To convert a pm time to 24-hour I...
I found... tricky today, so next time I will...

loText.walt: …read, write and convert time between analogue clocks, 12-hour and 24-hour digital formats.
loText.tiob: …being precise about time and moving between different ways of showing it is a skill we use every day.
loText.iwstb: …converting accurately between analogue and digital times, including the more challenging cases around noon and midnight.

spotTheMistake.concept: Confusing hours and minutes when reading an analogue clock
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: The clock shows 9:35
spotTheMistake.errorNote: The short hand (hours) points to 7 and the long hand (minutes) points to 9 — so the time is 7:45, not 9:35. The short hand tells you the hour; the long hand tells you the minutes.

lessons.li: Solve problems involving converting from hours to minutes, minutes to seconds, years to months, and weeks to days.
lessons.cycleNote: Pupils often confuse multiplication and division direction here. Cycle 1 establishes the conversion facts and applies them to simple problems. Cycle 2 moves to multi-step and reasoning.

cycle1.focus: Establishing conversion facts and applying them in one step
ido: Build the conversion facts table together: 60 seconds = 1 minute; 60 minutes = 1 hour; 24 hours = 1 day; 7 days = 1 week; 52 weeks ≈ 1 year; 12 months = 1 year; 365 days = 1 year.
Model: 'A film lasts 2 hours 35 minutes. How many minutes?' 2×60=120, 120+35=155 minutes. Then reverse: '175 minutes — how many hours and minutes?' 175÷60=2r55.
Common error: multiplying by 100 instead of 60. Address explicitly with a counterexample.
wedo: 8 one-step conversion problems on whiteboards — mix of units, mix of directions (to smaller units and to larger units). Pupils show working.
Pairs create their own problem for another pair to solve. Share two with the class.

roles: A: writes a one-step conversion problem
B: solves it showing working
C: verifies using the inverse operation
trios.task: Three rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Write a problem where the answer is exactly 2 hours. How many different problems can you find?

independent.standard: 10 one-step conversion problems covering all four unit relationships. Mix of directions.
independent.supported: 6 problems. Conversion facts reference card provided. Worked example for each direction.

ido: Time conversion facts
Converting in both directions
wedo: 8 conversion problems — show working
trios: Trios — write, solve, verify with inverse

cycle2.focus: Multi-step conversion problems; reasoning about precision
ido: Model a multi-step problem: 'How many seconds in 2 hours 15 minutes?' First convert hours to minutes (2×60=120, add 15 = 135 minutes), then to seconds (135×60=8100).
Discuss precision: '18 months — how many weeks?' 18 months = 1 year 6 months. 1 year ≈ 52 weeks, 6 months ≈ 26 weeks, total ≈ 78 weeks. Ask: 'Is this exact? Why not?'
wedo: Six multi-step problems. Pupils work in pairs, showing all steps. Share — compare methods. Is there a more efficient approach?
Estimation challenge: roughly how many seconds old are you? What assumptions are you making?

roles: A: writes a multi-step time problem using at least two unit conversions
B: solves it methodically
C: verifies using the inverse
trios.task: Two rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Write a problem that has two possible answers depending on whether you count leap years. Explain both.

independent.standard: Eight multi-step problems. Include: 'How many seconds in 3 minutes 20 seconds?' and 'How many months in 2½ years?' and one reasoning question about exact vs approximate conversions.
independent.supported: Five problems. Conversion facts card. Structured working frame: 'Step 1: convert ___ to ___. Step 2: convert ___ to ___.' Excludes year-to-month problems initially.

ido: Multi-step conversions
Exact vs approximate — when does it matter?
wedo: Six multi-step problems — compare methods

support: Conversion facts reference card for both cycles. Structured working frames for Cycle 2.
Cycle 1: one-step problems only. Cycle 2: two-step problems only; exclude years initially.
stretch: Write a problem requiring three different unit conversions to solve.
How many seconds have you been alive? Account for leap years. Show full working.

lp1.task: 10 one-step conversion problems covering all four unit relationships, mix of directions.

lp2.task: Eight multi-step conversion problems. Include reasoning about exact vs approximate conversions.

learningReviewStems: To convert from a larger unit to a smaller unit I...
I know to multiply and not divide here because...
A multi-step conversion problem needs...

loText.walt: …solve problems by converting between units of time.
loText.tiob: …time conversions use multiplication and division, and choosing the right operation is an important reasoning skill.
loText.iwstb: …solving one-step and multi-step conversion problems, showing clear working and checking using the reverse operation.

spotTheMistake.concept: Multiplying instead of dividing when converting to a smaller unit
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Convert 3 hours to minutes: 3 ÷ 60 = 0.05 minutes
spotTheMistake.errorNote: To go from a bigger unit (hours) to a smaller unit (minutes), you multiply: 3 × 60 = 180 minutes. Division is used when converting the other way — from minutes to hours.

lessons.li: Solve problems involving finding durations and end times, and read and interpret timetables.
lessons.cycleNote: Bridging through the hour is the critical skill in Cycle 1 — many pupils either add instead of subtract or forget to bridge. Cycle 2 applies this to real timetable contexts.

cycle1.focus: Finding durations and end/start times by bridging through the hour
ido: 'A baking show starts at 14:30 and lasts 1 hour 25 minutes. What time does it end?' Add hours first (14:30+1h=15:30), then minutes (15:30+25m=15:55). Write in both 12-hour and 24-hour.
Working backwards: 'A programme ends at 16:10 and lasted 50 minutes. When did it start?' 16:10−10m=16:00, 16:00−40m=15:20. Show the bridging step explicitly.
Key question: 'When do we need to bridge through the hour? When can we add/subtract directly?'
wedo: Six real-world problems (TV schedules, journey times, school day). Pupils solve on whiteboards showing: start time, method, end time.
Class compares methods: number line, column format, bridging through hours. Which is most reliable? Which is fastest?

roles: A: invents a real-world time problem
B: solves it on a number line
C: solves it using the column method — do they agree?
trios.task: Three rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Design a day's TV schedule with at least five programmes where no two overlap and total broadcast time is exactly 4 hours 30 minutes.

independent.standard: Eight contextualised duration and end/start time problems. Mix of finding end time, start time and duration. Include one requiring conversion to minutes to compare two durations.
independent.supported: Five problems — find the end time only. Number lines from 0–60 and a clock face provided.

ido: Finding durations and end times
Working backwards — when did it start?
wedo: Six real-world time problems
trios: Trios — invent, number line, column method

cycle2.focus: Reading and interpreting timetables; multi-step timetable problems
ido: Display a simplified bus timetable (four stops, five departure times). Model reading it: 'Which bus should I catch to arrive at Stop C by 10:00?' Scan the row, identify the latest valid departure.
'The 08:42 bus is 17 minutes late. What time does it arrive at Stop D?' Show the calculation. Discuss: why do public transport timetables use 24-hour time?
wedo: Pairs receive a train timetable. Three questions per pair — one reading, one duration, one multi-step problem. Share answers with another pair and resolve any disagreements.
Class problem: two friends leave from different stations on the same line. At what time and station do they first meet?

roles: A: finds the relevant row and column in the timetable
B: solves the problem
C: explains the solution in a full sentence including units
trios.task: Four questions from the timetable. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Plan a day trip using the timetable: leave after 09:00, return before 17:00, maximise time at the destination.

independent.standard: A timetable with five stops and six departure times. Eight questions ranging from simple reading to multi-step reasoning, including one where a connection is missed.
independent.supported: Simplified timetable (three stops, three times). Five questions. Relevant rows pre-highlighted.

ido: Reading a timetable
Multi-step timetable problem
wedo: Train timetable — three questions

support: Number lines and clock faces for Cycle 1. Pre-highlighted timetable rows for Cycle 2.
Cycle 1: find end time only. Cycle 2: simple reading questions only before multi-step.
stretch: Design a day trip plan using a real timetable. Calculate total travelling time and time at destination.
Write five timetable questions of your own, including one requiring multi-step reasoning.

lp1.task: Eight problems: find end time, start time and duration. Include one requiring conversion to minutes to compare durations.

lp2.task: A five-stop timetable. Eight questions from simple reading to multi-step reasoning, including a missed connection.

learningReviewStems: To find a duration I...
Bridging through the hour means...
When reading a timetable I always...

loText.walt: …calculate how long something takes and find start and end times, and read and use timetables.
loText.tiob: …working out durations and using timetables are skills we use in everyday life, from bus journeys to planning our day.
loText.iwstb: …finding start times, end times and durations accurately, and answering questions from a timetable including ones that need more than one step.

spotTheMistake.concept: Adding hours and minutes separately without bridging through the hour
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: A film starts at 2:45. It lasts 1 hour 30 minutes. It ends at 3:75.
spotTheMistake.errorNote: 3:75 is not a valid time — minutes only go up to 59. You need to bridge: 2:45 + 15 minutes = 3:00, then add the remaining 1 hour 15 minutes = 4:15.

lessons.li: Apply all time knowledge — mixed problem-solving across clock reading, unit conversion, durations and timetables.
lessons.cycleNote: Final lesson of the term. Assessment-style consolidation — pupils identify the problem type before selecting a method. Focus on metacognition and checking.

cycle1.focus: Identifying problem type and selecting the right method
ido: Display a 'Time Problem Board' with four problem types labelled: Clock Reading, Unit Conversion, Duration, Timetable. Model one from each type, narrating the identification step: 'I see a 24-hour time → Clock Reading. I see how long → Duration.'
Stress: read the problem fully before calculating. Identify what you are being asked to find. Choose the method. Show working. Check.
wedo: Four problems on the board, one from each type. Pairs identify the type and method before calculating. Share strategies.
Pupils mark their own work using the answer slide and identify which type they found hardest.

roles: A: reads the problem and identifies the type
B: solves it
C: checks using the inverse or an alternative method
trios.task: Four problems — one per type. Each pupil leads on one.
trios.challenge: Create a 'Time Escape Room' with four linked problems (one per type) where the answer to each is needed to unlock the next.

independent.standard: Eight problems across all four types. No worked examples. Pupils write the problem type, their chosen method, and the answer.
independent.supported: Six problems. Method selection card provided: 'If clocks → conversion. If how long → duration. If timetable → read carefully first.''

ido: Identify the problem type — then choose a method
wedo: Four problems — one per type

cycle2.focus: Multi-step and multi-type problems requiring combined skills
ido: Model a multi-type problem: 'A journey shown on an analogue clock starts at 10:50. The timetable says it takes 1 hour 35 minutes. What is the 24-hour arrival time?' Identify: need clock reading + duration + 24-hour conversion.
Stress: complex problems often require more than one skill. Break them into steps.
wedo: Three multi-type problems on whiteboards. Pupils identify all the skills needed before starting. Share their step plans before calculating.
Class discusses: 'What is a good checking strategy for these more complex problems?'

roles: A: writes a multi-type time problem requiring at least two different skills
B: solves it step by step
C: identifies each skill used and verifies
trios.task: Two rounds. Rotate.
trios.challenge: Design a 10-question time quiz for Year 4 covering all four types. Include an answer key and a mark scheme.

independent.standard: Four multi-type problems requiring combined skills. Pupils write which skills they used for each. Plus: design 4 questions for a time quiz — one per problem type.
independent.supported: Three multi-type problems with step-by-step working frames. Conversion reference card allowed.

ido: Multi-type problems — combining skills
wedo: Plan your steps before you calculate
independent: Independent — four multi-type problems and quiz design

support: Method selection card for Cycle 1. Step-by-step working frames for Cycle 2. Conversion reference card throughout.
Cycle 1: six problems only, single-type. Cycle 2: two multi-type problems with structured frames.
stretch: Design a 10-question time quiz for Year 4 with answer key and mark scheme.
Write a report: 'Everything a Year 4 pupil needs to know about time.' Include examples, common mistakes and strategies.

lp1.task: Eight mixed problems across all four time types. Write the problem type, method chosen and answer.

lp2.task: Four multi-type problems requiring combined skills. Write which skills were used. Design 4 quiz questions (one per type).

learningReviewStems: Before I start a time problem I...
The problem type I found most challenging today was... because...
A good checking strategy for complex time problems is...

loText.walt: …use all our time knowledge to solve a range of problems.
loText.tiob: …being able to spot what kind of problem it is and choose the right method shows real mathematical understanding.
loText.iwstb: …naming the problem type, choosing the correct method, showing my working and checking the answer for each problem.

spotTheMistake.concept: Using the wrong operation — subtracting arrival from departure for duration
spotTheMistake.errorInstruction: Train departs 09:20, arrives 11:05. Duration: 11:05 − 09:20 = 2:85
spotTheMistake.errorNote: 2:85 is not valid — you can't have 85 minutes. Find duration by bridging: 09:20 → 10:00 (40 min) → 11:05 (1h 5min) = 1 hour 45 minutes total.

keyQuestions.Coordinates: How can we describe exactly where something is?
keyQuestions.Symmetry: What makes a shape perfectly balanced?
keyQuestions.Time: How do we measure and compare time precisely?
